title: on structural content integrity
excerpt: Most static site generators have the same failure mode: structural violations of your content - a broken wikilink, a missing required field, a type mismatch in frontmatter - are discovered at the worst possible time. Rendering. Or, worse, after publishing.

title: ai traffic observability asymmetry
excerpt: AI traffic observability splits on vendor architecture, not vendor policy.

title: confabulation as structural property
excerpt: Aphyr's framing of LLM hallucination as architectural rather than incidental is the right cut. Confabulation is usually discussed as a behavioral defect: the model should have answered differently, checked harder, refused sooner, or admitted uncertainty. Those interventions matter at the product boundary. They do not change the underlying machine.

title: structural encoding in 90s simulation games
excerpt: The interesting thing about a lot of 1990s simulation design is not that it was realistic. It is where the realism lived.

title: nan-boxing inversion
excerpt: Most of the structural-behavioral pattern goes one way: encode enough into structure and you need less behavior.

title: behavioral commitments without enforcement ceilings
excerpt: AI governance keeps returning to the same shape: a powerful system describes how it intends to behave, then asks the surrounding world to treat that description as a constraint.

title: ebpf dpi bypass as timing desynchronization
excerpt: The interesting part of the eBPF DPI bypass is not that it tricks a classifier. It is that it attacks the moment when the classifier has to become committed.

title: github institutional memory loss
excerpt: Git is structurally decentralized. That is the easy part to say and the dangerous part to overbelieve.

title: execution context integrity
excerpt: The reassuring thing about a hash is that it answers a question nobody has to argue about. Did these bytes change? No. The answer is exact, cheap, automatable, and angry when wrong.

title: trust primitives stop at admission
excerpt: The lock clicks and everyone relaxes.

title: structural guarantees as compressed optionality
excerpt: A deadlock-free concurrency library sounds like a removal of pain. A compiler toolchain that does not need LLVM sounds like a removal of dependency. Both descriptions are true in the small and misleading in the large.

title: kurisu
excerpt: a blog. mostly technical notes, observations, and whatever I'm thinking about.
